intro_text:
Cottesloe Beach sits on the Indian Ocean coast of Western Australia, between Fremantle to the south and central Perth to the northeast, in the Town of Cottesloe. The sand spans roughly 1.5 kilometres between North Cottesloe Beach and Mosman Park Beach. Cottesloe has been one of Western Australia's most-used beaches since the suburb was established in the 1880s, and the Cottesloe Surf Life Saving Club, which opened in 1909, was the first lifesaving club in the state.

The beach is backed by a row of Norfolk Island pines along Marine Parade and a set of terraced limestone lawns that step down toward the water. Above the sand stands the Indiana Teahouse, a colonial-revival building completed in 1996 to a design by architect Laurie Scanlan. It takes its name from an earlier Indiana Tea House that operated on the site from 1913 to 1970, and its form references the Centenary Bathing Pavilion that stood here from 1929 until it was demolished in 1982. The Indiana was added to the State Register of Heritage Places on 8 June 2021.

Offshore stands the Cottesloe pylon, a concrete post built in 1935 to anchor a shark net after a fatal attack on swimmer Simeon Ettelson in November 1925. The full enclosure was never finished; a May 1936 storm dislodged the inner pylon under the jetty, leaving the single northern pylon that remains. Its spike has been knocked off in storms more than once, including in 1995 and again in 2009, and a restoration costing about A$172,000 was approved in December 2008.

The beach is also the site of Sculpture by the Sea, Cottesloe, established in 2005 as a companion to the original Bondi exhibition. Held each March, it lines the sand and lawns with more than 70 works and is billed as the world's largest free-to-the-public sculpture exhibition. The 2026 edition ran from 5 to 22 March. Cottesloe is open ocean rather than a sheltered bay, and it has a real shark history: on 6 November 2000, Ken Crew was killed by a great white in shallow water at the northern end during a dawn swim, an attack witnessed from the shore that reshaped how Western Australia talks about its beaches.
honest_reckoning_note:
Cottesloe is open Indian Ocean, not a netted bay, and the marketing rarely says so plainly. The pylon offshore exists because a shark killed Simeon Ettelson here in November 1925; the 1935 concrete post is the leftover of a shark-net scheme that was never completed and that a 1936 storm broke apart. Seventy-five years after Ettelson, on 6 November 2000, Ken Crew was killed by a great white in waist-deep water at the northern end of the beach during an early-morning swim, in front of people on the shore. The water that makes Cottesloe a genuine ocean beach rather than a paddling pool is the same water that carries that risk.

The second honest point is about the buildings. The Indiana Teahouse reads as old but was finished in 1996, designed to evoke the 1929 Centenary Pavilion that the council had demolished in 1982. Its colonial-revival styling, its lease arrangements, and a 2025 proposal to add a sauna have all drawn local argument about who the foreshore is for. Cottesloe is a working civic beach that does a lot of performing — of heritage it rebuilt, and of safety on an ocean that has twice proven it is not tame.

day_in_time.dawn: Dawn is the swimmers' hour. Groups gather on the terraced lawns and head out from the northern end toward the pylon while the Norfolk pines are still dark against the sky; the 2000 Crew attack happened at 6:20 a.m. on exactly this kind of dawn swim, and the dawn squads have continued regardless.
day_in_time.midday: By the middle of the day the limestone terraces and the sand fill with families, and the lifeguards patrol the flagged area in front of the Indiana Teahouse. The open-ocean swell, larger here than in Perth's sheltered river beaches, breaks across the main sand.
day_in_time.golden: Late afternoon is the postcard hour, when low sun comes in off the Indian Ocean behind the pylon and lights the row of Norfolk Island pines along Marine Parade. Cottesloe faces west, so the sun sets straight out to sea over the water.
day_in_time.night: After dark the foreshore stays lit and used, with the Indiana Teahouse and the Marine Parade venues holding the crowd. During the March Sculpture by the Sea run, some of the works are lit after sundown and the lawns stay busy into the evening.

food_drink.name: Indiana Teahouse
food_drink.description: The colonial-revival landmark above the sand, completed in 1996 to a Laurie Scanlan design and heritage-listed in 2021. The building has changed operators over the years; check current tenancy before planning a meal around it.
food_drink.where: 91 Marine Parade, directly above the main beach

food_drink.name: Marine Parade venues
food_drink.description: The strip facing the beach carries cafes, a hotel pub, and casual food outlets within a short walk of the sand and the surf club. Prices run from casual takeaway to sit-down dining with an ocean view.

timeline.title: Cottesloe established and the beach takes off
timeline.description: The suburb of Cottesloe is established in the 1880s, and the beach becomes one of the most-used in Western Australia from that decade onward.

timeline.title: Cottesloe Surf Life Saving Club opens
timeline.description: The Cottesloe Surf Life Saving Club holds its official opening, the first lifesaving club in Western Australia.

timeline.title: The original Indiana Tea House opens
timeline.description: An Indiana Tea House begins operating on the foreshore site; it runs until 1970 and lends its name to the later building.

timeline.title: Fatal shark attack on Simeon Ettelson
timeline.description: Swimmer Simeon Ettelson is killed by a shark at Cottesloe, the attack that prompts the shark-net and pylon scheme of the following decade.
timeline.source: Wikipedia: Cottesloe Beach; inHerit (Cottesloe Beach Pylon)

timeline.title: Centenary Bathing Pavilion built
timeline.description: An elegant bathing pavilion, the result of an architectural competition, is completed in 1929 and serves as the icon of the state's most popular beach.

timeline.title: The Cottesloe pylon is built
timeline.description: A concrete pylon is built offshore to anchor a shark net. The full enclosure is never completed; a May 1936 storm dislodges the inner pylon, leaving only the northern post that survives today.
timeline.source: Wikipedia: Cottesloe Beach; inHerit (Cottesloe Beach Pylon)

timeline.title: Centenary Pavilion demolished
timeline.description: The 1929 Centenary Bathing Pavilion is demolished, removing the foreshore landmark that the Indiana Teahouse would later be designed to echo.

timeline.title: Indiana Teahouse completed
timeline.description: The current Indiana Teahouse, designed by architect Laurie Scanlan in a colonial-revival style that references the demolished Centenary Pavilion, is completed and opens.

timeline.title: Ken Crew killed by a great white
timeline.description: On the morning of 6 November 2000, Ken Crew is killed by a great white shark in shallow water at the northern end of the beach during a dawn swim, witnessed from the shore.

timeline.title: Sculpture by the Sea, Cottesloe begins
timeline.description: Sculpture by the Sea launches a Cottesloe edition, a companion to the Bondi exhibition that began in 1997. It runs each March and grows into the world's largest free sculpture exhibition.
timeline.source: Wikipedia: Sculpture by the Sea; sculpturebythesea.com

timeline.title: Indiana Teahouse heritage-listed
timeline.description: The Indiana Teahouse is entered on the State Register of Heritage Places on 8 June 2021, reference 16637.

zones.name: Marine Parade and the Norfolk pines
zones.character: The promenade behind the beach, lined with Norfolk Island pines and stepping down to the sand over terraced limestone lawns. The Indiana Teahouse anchors its centre.
zones.best_for: The defining promenade view and sunset over the Indian Ocean
zones.notes: Faces due west; this is the row of pines that appears on Perth's postcards and marketing.

zones.name: The Cottesloe pylon
zones.character: The lone concrete pylon offshore, built 1935 as the surviving anchor of an unfinished shark-net enclosure. A swimming waypoint and a fixed point in every long-lens photograph of the beach.
zones.best_for: Long-lens photography and a swim target for strong swimmers
zones.notes: Its spike has been knocked off in storms, including 1995 and 2009; restored from 2008.

zones.name: Northern end
zones.character: The quieter northern stretch toward North Cottesloe, used by the dawn swim groups heading for the pylon. Rockier and less developed than the central sand.
zones.best_for: Early swims and a thinner crowd
zones.notes: The 6 November 2000 fatal shark attack on Ken Crew occurred at the northern end during a dawn swim.

zones.name: The patrolled main beach
zones.character: The central sand in front of the Indiana Teahouse and the surf club, flagged and patrolled in season, where families and the bulk of visitors gather.
zones.best_for: Patrolled swimming and family beach days
zones.notes: Site of the March Sculpture by the Sea installations across the sand and lawns.

landmarks.name: Indiana Teahouse
landmarks.description: Colonial-revival foreshore building completed in 1996, named after an Indiana Tea House that operated on the site from 1913 to 1970 and designed to echo the demolished 1929 Centenary Bathing Pavilion. Added to the State Register of Heritage Places on 8 June 2021.

landmarks.name: Cottesloe pylon
landmarks.description: Concrete pylon standing offshore, built in 1935 to anchor a shark net after the 1925 fatal attack on Simeon Ettelson. The enclosure was never completed and a 1936 storm dislodged the inner pylon, leaving this single northern post as an icon of the beach.

landmarks.name: Norfolk Island pines, Marine Parade
landmarks.description: The row of Norfolk Island pines (Araucaria heterophylla) along Marine Parade behind the terraced lawns, the signature backdrop of Cottesloe and a fixture of Perth's visual identity.

cultural_refs.title: Sculpture by the Sea, Cottesloe
cultural_refs.description: Annual March outdoor exhibition established at Cottesloe in 2005 as a companion to the Bondi original, lining the sand and lawns with more than 70 works and billed as the world's largest free-to-the-public sculpture exhibition.

cultural_refs.title: The 2000 Ken Crew shark attack
cultural_refs.description: The fatal great-white attack on Ken Crew in shallow water at the northern end on 6 November 2000, witnessed from the shore, a defining event in Western Australia's public conversation about ocean safety and shark policy.

recurring_events.name: Sculpture by the Sea, Cottesloe
recurring_events.when_text: Each March; the 2026 edition ran 5–22 March
recurring_events.description: Outdoor sculpture exhibition across the beach, terraces, and lawns, free to the public, featuring more than 70 works by artists from Australia and abroad. Established 2005.

things_to_know.label: Open ocean
things_to_know.note: Cottesloe faces the open Indian Ocean, not a sheltered bay. It is patrolled in season, but it has a genuine shark history — fatal attacks in 1925 and 2000.

things_to_know.label: Getting there
things_to_know.note: The Transperth Fremantle line stops at Cottesloe station, about a 10–15 minute walk downhill to the sand; the beach is roughly 10 km southwest of central Perth.

things_to_know.label: The pylon swim
things_to_know.note: Swimming out to the 1935 pylon is a long-standing local fitness ritual for strong swimmers; it is well offshore and not a casual paddle.

things_to_know.label: March crowds
things_to_know.note: Sculpture by the Sea fills the beach and lawns for roughly three weeks each March; expect heavy foot traffic and limited parking during the run.

access_note: Cottesloe is about 10 km southwest of central Perth and north of Fremantle. The Transperth Fremantle railway line stops at Cottesloe station, roughly a 10–15 minute walk from the beach; Marine Parade carries the foreshore parking, which fills early on summer weekends and during the March sculpture exhibition.
spike_explainer:
Cottesloe is Perth's civic ocean beach, and almost everything that makes it recognizable is layered onto a single west-facing strip of Indian Ocean coast between Fremantle and the city. Strip away the postcard and what remains is roughly 1.5 kilometres of sand that has been the state's gathering beach since the 1880s, fronting genuinely open water rather than a sheltered bay. Onto that base, four things have been stacked over more than a century, and the stack is the story.

**The pines and the terraces.** The first layer is the promenade. A row of Norfolk Island pines runs along Marine Parade above a set of terraced limestone lawns that step down to the sand, and that silhouette — dark conifers against a flat western horizon — has become shorthand for Perth itself. It faces due west, so the sun sets straight out to sea, and the late light off the water behind the trees is the frame the city has used to sell itself for decades.

**The Teahouse that performs its own age.** The second layer is the Indiana Teahouse, the colonial-revival building above the main sand. It looks like it has presided over the beach for a hundred years; it was finished in 1996. Its name comes from an earlier Indiana Tea House that ran on the site from 1913 to 1970, and architect Laurie Scanlan designed the new building to echo the Centenary Bathing Pavilion that stood here from 1929 until the council demolished it in 1982. So the most photographed structure on the beach is a 1990s reconstruction of a memory — heritage rebuilt rather than preserved, and added to the State Register of Heritage Places in 2021 on those terms.

**The pylon and the reason it exists.** The third layer stands offshore: a lone concrete pylon, built in 1935. It is not decorative. It was meant to anchor a shark net after a shark killed swimmer Simeon Ettelson at Cottesloe in November 1925. The enclosure was never completed, and a May 1936 storm dislodged the inner pylon under the jetty, leaving the single northern post that survives. Its spike has been knocked off in storms more than once — in 1995 and again in 2009 — and it was restored from 2008. Today it reads as an icon and a swim target; it began as a safety structure that failed to get finished.

**The sculpture and the show.** The fourth layer is annual rather than fixed. Since 2005, every March, Sculpture by the Sea, Cottesloe lines the sand, terraces, and lawns with more than 70 works and runs free to the public, billed as the largest exhibition of its kind in the world. For three weeks the civic beach becomes an open-air gallery, and the same pines-and-pylon frame that sells Perth becomes a backdrop for contemporary sculpture. The 2026 edition ran 5–22 March.

**The honest base under the layers.** All four layers sit on open Indian Ocean, and that is the part the marketing tends to soften. The pylon exists because the water killed someone in 1925; on 6 November 2000, a great white killed Ken Crew in shallow water at the northern end during a dawn swim, in front of people on the beach. Cottesloe is a real ocean beach — patrolled, beloved, and not tame. The pines, the rebuilt Teahouse, and the March sculptures are the civic performance; the water underneath is the fact they are performed on.

key_facts.label: Location
key_facts.value: Indian Ocean coast, between Fremantle and Perth, Town of Cottesloe, WA

key_facts.label: Beach length
key_facts.value: About 1.5 km, North Cottesloe to Mosman Park

key_facts.label: The pylon
key_facts.value: Built 1935 to anchor an unfinished shark net

margin_notes.text: The Indiana Teahouse name is older than the building: an original Indiana Tea House ran on the site from 1913 to 1970. The current structure, finished in 1996, borrows the name and the look of the 1929 Centenary Pavilion the council had pulled down in 1982.

The pylon is a safety relic. It was built in 1935 to anchor a shark net after Simeon Ettelson was killed here in 1925. The net was never completed, and a 1936 storm wrecked the inner pylon — the surviving post is the half-finished plan, frozen in concrete.

Sculpture by the Sea began at Sydney's Bondi in 1997; the Cottesloe edition followed in 2005. Held every March, it is billed as the world's largest free-to-the-public sculpture exhibition, with 70-plus works on the sand and lawns.

The Norfolk Island pines along Marine Parade are Araucaria heterophylla, native to a tiny Pacific island and planted as a salt-tolerant amenity tree across southern Australian foreshores. Their hard symmetrical silhouette is what makes the Cottesloe skyline read instantly.

On 6 November 2000, Ken Crew was killed by a great white in waist-deep water at the northern end at about 6:20 a.m. during a dawn swim, witnessed from the shore. It remains the defining shark event of the beach and shaped WA's later shark-mitigation policy.

Cottesloe faces due west into the open Indian Ocean, so unlike Perth's river beaches the sun sets straight out to sea here. That orientation is why the pines-and-water sunset became the city's standard postcard.

reckoning_pullquote: The water that makes Cottesloe a genuine ocean beach is the same water that killed Simeon Ettelson in 1925 and Ken Crew in 2000.
